I find it ironic that this is the most scuffed and janky backroom game I've played among the popular ones on steam, yet I had the most fun on it with some friends. As always with these kinds of games, I think they truly shine with friends with the use of proximity chat.   Unlike other backroom games this one wasn't either extremely easy or unfairly difficult, many of the levels felt balanced, with each level having unique mechanics within the entities themselves or their rooms. This game handles safe rooms in a better way then other backroom games, allowing entities to go through doors into the safe rooms as a punishment if you don't close the doors, while ensuring they actually wonder off. Some levels changes things up by making you crouch under objects or into holes, to hide in a locker, or by removing safe zones entirely. Dying feels fair as the entities actually makes noises, queuing unsuspected players. With most chases that end with the entity catching up with the player if they don't utilize the safe zones.  On Normal Difficulty you only lose a little bit of progress if everyone dies, as there are multiple checkpoints within the levels, and your progress on the tasks are kept. You lose items if everyone dies, but each level has plenty of loot at their checkpoints. As long as one person passes a room, everyone passes. This allows for a more casual experience, your friends can goof around, and anyone even playing solo can finish the game without it being very difficult. The game has more difficulty options, so if this seems too easy for you, nightmare mode is one click away.   As for the game's problems, it is very scuffed and quite janky. While a lot of it brings a charm and is kinda funny, some of it can cause frustrations within playthroughs. For starters trying to hold or consume items in your hands half the time doesn't work, forcing you to switch back in forth in your hotbar, and in some occasions making you die if you can't drink that almond water or turn on that flashlight in time. In certain spots on certain maps entities can kill you through walls, sometimes the camera will get stuck when spectating, your camera can get stuck if you board the orange boat raft, it is possible to go out of bounds on certain maps (this one is fun), a lot of the animations in the game look a little off and goofy, yet brings that charm.   The biggest issue I had with this game was the 'Fun Level'. The game doesn't really explain what you are supposed to do on the level, which will cause you to die a lot trying to figure out what you are doing wrong. If you make too much noise, the entities will chase and kill you, it explains that, but if you cross their line of sight, even while crouching, they will kill you. Since it doesn't explain that last part players will assume they are blind, when they are not. It also doesn't help that a lot of the fun entities are angled where they will see you before you really see them, causing them to all chase you leaving you clueless as to why. You are also supposed to aggro them by popping the balloons, so that they can chase you, and then return to their positions at a different angle, allowing you to progress... Yet never explains that, and that design honestly doesn't really make sense. After learning what you are supposed to do, the level was one of the more fun ones, but was initially frustrating from the lack of direction on what exactly you are supposed to do.   Overall I would recommend this backrooms game, especially if you have a group of friends to play with. Whether you take the game seriously, or goof off with some friends, you will mostly have fun. This game has a surprising amount of content compared to other backroom games, it has a lot of levels, it has multiple endings, secret levels, and various game modes if you are left wanting a challenge. Doing about everything on Normal Difficulty it took me and my friends ~ 11 hours of playtime. If you are deciding between other backroom games on Steam, I would go with this one.

Probably the best Backrooms game overall, I've had it since it came out in late 2022, so its been awesome seeing this game evolve from a shorter and buggy game and developing into a more quality, polished, and content packed experience. This is a fun game to play solo, and an even better game to play co-op with your friends, and the speed running experience is pretty epic overall if you have beaten it multiple times and want to challenge yourself. A lot of attention to detail, and I'm sure the game will continue to get great updates in the near future as it has for the last few years. With the Backrooms movie out, more people are flocking to Backrooms games in great numbers, including this one. I'm sure they will enjoy this one as I have. Shoutout to Fancy and the others involved in making this game so great!

Bought it to play with friends with high expectations. Game completely failed to create immersion. Overly reused assets, nonsense puzzle design, janky movement and mechanics, terrible animations, corny sound design, don't even get me started on the monster design, no the black spaghetti man is not scary. The devs don't really understand what's scary about liminal spaces and failed to capitalize on that fear, they let the monsters do the work instead, it didn't work. I very well know that I haven't gone through all the levels, but it really doesn't seem like its gonna get any better. It just got more and more annoying over time. If you buy this game, just know that you're not here to explore and experience haunting liminal spaces, you're here to be run down by a generic monster inside some unity assets thrown together randomly, while also having to constantly look for almond water cans that completely defeat the purpose of wearing a hazmat suit so that somehow you regain sanity, are almonds super antidepressants now? Makes no sense, wont be losing any sleep to this. I guess it could be fun for some people that want more action instead of exploration, but even that could've been done better. performance's also quite bad, too many frame drops.

This game is an affront to the backrooms and video games in general.   As a backrooms game, it is no more than obtuse puzzles that waste your time and give no stimulation. Every level is either: find a some random assortment of things while hiding from monsters who are all the same, or fumble around in a maze until you find an exit. This game could've at least done the cliche monsters that can't hear, monsters that can't see, or monsters that can smell or something. Instead, its just some hodge-podge model made in by an amateur blender user that just run at you and scream. The actual backrooms themselves arent even in this game for more than 5 minutes.  As a horror game, this game will not scare you anymore than a jack in the box. Every monster uses default unity animations so they all run like stilted mannequins. The only time this game got any reactions out of me was when the broken AI of the monsters suddenly made them sit behind corners, or when the braindead gameplay made me blow a gasket.  This game also has horrid optimization. Tiny springs of water will make your camera skip frames and make you slam your head into a wall. Every time a new level loads, the game turns super choppy.   This is what happens when a """""developer""""" who really only watched some basic unity tutorials about UI elements and loading screens decides to hop on a dead bandwagon, and turn a bunch of video game levels written by children into an actual game. Then that dev puts it into """""early access"""" so that it can sit in your steam library for 3 years so you can't get a refund it. If you want a backrooms experience, you're better off looking for a roblox game.  If you want to experience this game for completely free, get a friend to hide a grape in your house, turn off all the lights, and try to find it. Then call it level 238213904: "Get ♥♥♥♥♥♥ in mr. biggles house" and spend 5 minutes dragging premade unity assets and put it on steam for 1000x its worth.

The biggest thing - this game is more frustrating than it is scary or fun  This person said it best. "Visually the game is fantastic, it is perhaps the most "accurate" portrayal of the backrooms I have seen in any game, and looks stunning without killing my frame rate issues.  Gameplay-wise, it is a game I wouldn't recommend anyone buy for $9.99. In short, the game simply lacks good game design. A couple of examples would be the ending of the party room, in which the player is meant to hide under the table to make their way across the room full of enemies. You are able to barely make it if you time everything properly. The result of you failing is getting to slowly make your way through the level again. This is one of the examples I see as the dev equating hard for no reason with "challenging," and challenging inherently being worth the effort.  The End is a level that made me go on my own philosophical journey about game design as I died again and again to the monster. This level simply lacks any kind of gimmick or really any kind of gameplay mechanic at all. Playing with friends, we tried many different things from moving slowly, running fast, standing still if it saw us, etc. The fact of the matter is, this is just a monster that roams, and if it sees you, you die. No place to hide, no way to outrun it, if it sees you, you die. And if there is a gameplay mechanic, it just does not work,  I think the issue with a lot of indie game devs is that in their minds, making the game hard = making the game fun. But there is no satisfaction in beating a hard level if it makes you think you should have beaten it 20 attempts ago. When done well, a challenging game makes the player think the inability to win is a flaw of their own they need to overcome. In this game, I can't help but see it as a flaw in the game."

It's alright at times but it could be way better if given some love.  The game mixes puzzles, sanity drain, stealth, and gimmicks together where you kind of forget your in the backrooms. I am playing it with a group of friends and we are constantly finding ourselves struggling with sections due poor enemy ai, janky door physics, and down right terrible stealth mechanics.   Theirs a level that is filled with enemies that need to by snuck past. You are forced to break stealth in order to aggro them, after which they are supposed to return to their positions but with their backs turned so you can crouch past them. The problem is that they often simply dont return to their positions and just stand there blocking the narrow path, or kill the player that aggroed them while they are hiding (which they arent supposed to do), and even if those things don't occur, they may still just randomly aggro on a lone sneaking player who is behind cover and out of sight. We beat this level after about an hour of trial and error when my friend got irritated and just sprinted, angering like 15 entities, and getting omega lucky with finding the exit.  Skin Stealers are an incredibly annoying enemy. For some reason, the devs made them almost the same speed as the player, making them virtually impossible to shake off of your tail unless you get behind a door. The issue is, it takes a whole 2 seconds for doors to open and close, making pulling that off frustratingly difficult. We've gotten better at this, but theirs a lot of luck involved.   Another annoying aspect is that clients (non hosts) lack certain features. Some puzzles seemed to only work when I input the solution and certain noises simply don't play for my friends. The first level has some audio cues that let you know an enemy is near. Despite my friends sticking to my side like glue, I heard these audio cues while they didn't.  The sanity system is also just... not it. Levels where you sanity drains slowly have an abundance of almond waters, whereas levels that drain it quickly have 2 or 3 that need to last your entire team for the duration of the level. They also only seem to refill about 50 percent sanity which sucks. Compounding this is the fact that you drop loot on death and said loot despawns. So if your in level A that has a bunch of sanity meds then go to Level B which has very little while being a challenging area, your likely just going to wipe and lose everything as you learn the level naturally.   Ultimately, of all of the systems and mechanics listed above, the game does none of them well. You go in expecting a scary backrooms game with friends but end up playing tug-of-war with the games crappy mechanics instead. The game becomes a lot less scary when your irritated and my friends and I get irritated with it frequently. I think with some enemy tuning, significantly improved stealth, and more levels where the goal is to simply find your way through the labyrinth, this game could be pretty decent. The bones are here but its just not good as is imo. My friends an I aren't finished with it and if their are some major sections later on that change my view, I will update my guide. The fact it has full cross play is a huge W however.  Also, this is very subjective but I find the enemy models to be kind of goofy. The first enemy is spooky but most of the others we have encountered have a look that just seems like they were sculpted from clay. Their animations are stiff as well. This is obviously an indie game so I don't think I'd dock points for this — They're good enough. I just don't find them scary, but I am harder to scare generally so that may just be a me thing.

Only the first level is reminiscent of The Backrooms, and that level only lasts a few minutes. Pointless cash-grab of a game.  Escape The Backrooms is a low effort puzzle game, where a thing chases you with super loud screams and kills you in a single hit. Very one dimensional. Every level is essentially 'Grab key and get to door' or 'turn on switch and get to door'. There isn't anything impressive or fun here. The game isn't scary, even when playing solo. Its genuinely worse than most Roblox horror games I played as a kid, as those are free, more original, and are typically higher effort.   You can break the AI super easily, and in the almost two hours I played, I ran into multiple game breaking bugs. Invisible entities, entities not being aggressive, entities teleporting/spawning on top of the player, constant desync in multiplayer, and doors glitching out and breaking just to name a few.   Overall, 1/10. Not worth your time or hard-drive space. Don't buy on sale either. You'd be better off playing The Outlast Trials, SCP Containment Breach, or Phasmophobia if you want a horror game with co-op.   Fun Fact: Only 47% of people who bought this even launched and played the first level, which is about five minutes long. Less than 35% of players have ever seen level two (Based on Steam Global Achievements).

i play a lot of backrooms games. i mean it; a lot. ive played at least ten in just recent memory, probably closer to twenty. this is the worst one ive played.  the game's vision is there, but its gameplay is not. lets go through a few issues ive experienced just in the first few chapters:  the garage entity's ai. if you hide in the wrong room, youre basically (or genuinely are) stuck there. the entity will camp the door until it either gets bored (which can take minutes) or until you try to leave, where it will kill you. if you try to hide in the elevator the ai also just completely bugs out.  a literal curb. as it turns out, the player cannot walk up a curb; they need a jump AND momentum. seems like a "whatever" until you're getting chased by the garage entity and realize he can just walk up while you cant. this did actually happen to me and i lost every bit of my being  the puzzles. every single puzzle in this game follows a similar idea: either its impossible as trying to break into area 51 with footage, or its easier than taking a breath. some puzzles are completely unreasonable for the average person to figure out, and i garauntee any streamers or other content creators playing this were carried by their chat or comments.  loading screens. to help with lag (which is still VERY present in multiplayer when not hosting, but thats a different point), the game divides its areas with loading screens. sounds great, of course... but its also a curse i wouldnt wish upon my biggest haters. why? well, after successfully getting through the garage, descending to the basement and escaping that too, the player arrives at a gate. they walk through a door into a big tunnel. this big tunnel contains a door that leads to a room with a computer with an application called "gate.exe." the player opens this application and is told a gate has opened. if they choose to go back through the door they came through to get to the tunnel, they will find the gate; however, this works completely different in multiplayer. EVERYONE has to go back through the door. so what's the issue? when going through the door back the way one came, they see an identical looking room aside from one missing button. in this case, its really easy to assume that the game is broken, because an average player would just see the gate not open.  another problem about the garage entity. in the basement, there is also this entity, which would be fine if closing doors on it worked, but it doesnt half of the time. if the entity is too close, which it probably will be unless you were already super far away from it, it can get through the door while its a sliver open. if someone asks for proof, i can provide them with video proof.    tldr: i would give this game a yes if it wasnt a nesting place for glitches. a bit of pest control goes a long way.

This game PMO. Graphics are definitely a "meh" here. Player models (or rather animations) are... yeah. One-shot monsters that walk from behind places where it's not possible to notice them... on a regular basis. Most quests are either insanely obvious and redundant, completely unclear if you're playing for the first time, or "find 3 keys, turn 3 valves, pull 3 levers, press 3 buttons" and etc. Levels give you 0 agency. On the fun level you have to do the EXACT sequence of actions if you want ANY chance to finish it. No idea how people completed it without a wiki. In the pipelines (idk what that area is called) you can run away from the monster, hide in the area before the station level and walk back to try to enter the fun level. Then the monster spawns there regardless. Even though it just ran past you in the opposite direction. ZERO agency. Terror hotel boiler room has got to be the most boring maze in existence. The game is also pretty buggy. Monsters notice you when they realistically shouldn't. They kill you through walls on occasion. The dogs in the station sometimes refuse to run away and attack you (I guess that's predictable enough from the posters but it's not how they usually actually behave). Fun level is definitely the worst, since partygoer logic makes no sense. Yeah, the monster standing with its back to me will notice me but the one looking right at me? No issue walking in front of it. My friend also found a way to infinitely sprint, which is probably the only bug actually improving the game. Graphics (other than the player) aren't particularly noteworthy, not the worst, far from the best. Few issues with stretched UVs or overlapping meshes, but fine aside from that. The positives: there are a few decent levels. The one with the hills is pretty good. Grassrooms is nice. The one with the negative level 0 is probably the only one with interesting mechanics. There are probably the most levels of any backrooms game, but then again, what's the point if they suck? Is it fun? My take - hard NO.

I honestly don’t understand how Escape the Backrooms is so popular when the game runs like absolute garbage half the time. Me and my friends tried playing it because everyone kept saying it was “super scary” and “really fun,” but the scariest part was trying to get more than 20 FPS without my computer sounding like it was about to explode. The graphics are not even that crazy either, so I seriously don’t know why the game is so unoptimized. Games that look ten times better somehow run smoother than this.    Every time we loaded into a new level, somebody’s game would lag out or freeze for no reason. Sometimes textures wouldn’t even load properly and it looked like the walls were made out of blurry soup. And don’t even get me started on the lighting because somehow turning around too fast makes the whole game stutter like crazy. I literally had moments where the monster wasn’t even scary because it was moving at like 5 frames per second. It looked more goofy than terrifying.    The multiplayer is also a mess. One second your friend is next to you and then suddenly they teleport across the map because the game can’t handle basic connection stuff. We kept getting random lag spikes where doors wouldn’t open, items wouldn’t pick up, and sometimes voice chat would just completely die. It ruins the whole atmosphere when everyone is yelling “WAIT I’M LAGGING” every five minutes.    Another thing that annoys me is how long the loading screens are. Why does it take forever just to load into a level that’s mostly empty hallways? It’s literally yellow walls and carpets. I could understand if the game had giant detailed maps with insane graphics, but it doesn’t. Half the levels are just repeating rooms over and over again, so there’s no reason for my PC to struggle this hard.    The optimization settings barely help too. Lowering the graphics feels useless because the game still stutters no matter what you do. It’s like the developers forgot to actually optimize anything before releasing updates. My friend has a really good PC and even he was getting random frame drops for no reason, so it’s obviously not just me.    Also the game has this problem where everything feels delayed. Sometimes sprinting feels weird, doors take forever to react, and picking stuff up feels clunky. I don’t know if it’s because the servers are bad or the game engine is struggling, but it makes the whole experience annoying instead of scary. Horror games are supposed to feel smooth so you can actually get immersed, not like you’re fighting your computer the entire time.    I wanted to like Escape the Backrooms because the idea is actually cool, but the optimization completely ruins it for me. Instead of being scared, I spent most of the game complaining about lag, stuttering, freezing, and broken multiplayer. It honestly feels like the game needed way more time before being released because right now it feels unfinished. If the developers actually fixed the performance problems, the game could probably be way better, but currently it just feels like a badly optimized mess with monsters in it.

Every review for this game seems to be written by an illiterate child, so I thought I would step in and write something legitimately cohesive. For those looking for the CliffNotes, here it is: This game is full of so many confounding oversights it is genuinely unfun to play. I'm not playing the game, nor am I enjoying the experience; [i]I'm putting up with the game[/i], so much so that it becomes a slogfest to get through. The game is less about how much fun you can have, and more about how much your patience will be tested.  	Before even starting a match, the default controls make absolutely no sense and lack any consistency, to the point where it seems like the developers have zero knowledge or experience in basic game design. For some reason, Tab is prioritized as the player list, while opening the inventory (one of the most important and frequently used functions), gets thrown to the wayside, bound to I. How in the world does it make sense to require a player to move his hand off either the mouse or WASD to access the inventory is beyond me. Once the horrible out-of-the-box experience is rectified by spending several minutes fixing the controls, however, it doesn't get any better. The only way to close the inventory, regardless of control scheme, is to press the Escape key. So while the developers pretend you can remap the controls, the reality is much different, and the constant cat-and-mouse of figuring out which controls are consistent and which ones are not quickly becomes exhausting. This problem is only cemented deeper through the equally problematic graphics.  	From a visual standpoint, the game is a disaster in its own right. Animations are extremely archaic and unpolished. Even with the graphics settings completely maxxed out, some textures are arbitrarily pixelated and low quality, while models and decals will frequently pop in and out to such an abhorrent degree that it's unforgivably distracting. Furthermore, the graphics quality ties into more than just the texture resolution and lighting. The art style itself is quite boring and generic, with no flair or visual substance that could potentially differentiate it in the crowded Backrooms genre. This problem is compounded by the fact that it's nearly impossible to tell what can and cannot be interacted with, tying back once again to the apparent ineptitude of the developers. In a well-developed video game, intractable objects will be quite obvious and apparent to the player, and how the developers approach this depends on the type of experience they seek to make, whether through tutorials, clever level design, or visual queues. Unfortunately, the developers of Escape the Backrooms chose none of those options and instead would rather have the player running around while spamming his interact key.  	Unlike any good video game on planet earth, the developers of Escape the Backrooms refuse to provide any help, tips, or objectives. You are quite literally thrown into the game and are expected to figure every single level out completely on your own or die trying. You might expect something reasonable, like maybe there's only assistance on the easiest difficulty, but there is no handholding whatsoever. The closest you get to help are tiny pieces of paper that have incredibly generic tips like, "don't look at the monster," or, "stay in the light." Unless you have a teammate to lend help or a third party guide, hours will be wasted searching for the objective and then figuring out how to complete it, which feels less like a well-designed experience and more like a subtle way to artificially pad out the runtime. A video game that throws you to the wolves is not good game design. Gently guiding the player through helpful, but not overbearing objectives, or teaching through example is.  	One of, if not the most important aspect of any Backrooms game is the level design. Unfortunately, the game completely drops the ball here, too. Some levels are so stale and devoid of substance they quite literally take thirty seconds to a minute to complete. Only a small fraction of the game is randomly generated, meaning the replayability and exploration, two key features of any solid survival game experience, die off quite quickly. All entrances, exits, spawn locations for the player, and the majority of items are identical every single run. This is a classic case quantity over quality. It's one thing to brag about having thirty distinct levels in your game, but it's entirely another when half those levels offer very little in terms of actual gameplay, and are fundamentally interchangeable every playthough.  	For a game that's been out for nearly a year, the sheer amount of bugs and glitches is appalling. When interacting with computers or other objects, it is not uncommon to get stuck during the interaction, essentially softlocking the player. The cursor will arbitrarily disappear, so critical points in the game where codes need to be entered become nearly impossible. When typing in a password using the keyboard, the game will frequently ignore its own dialogue box, preventing text from being entered whatsoever, resulting in more softlocks. Considering all these issues presented themselves after only two hours of gameplay, I'm scared of just how crummy and unpolished the rest of the levels must be.  	I suppose none of these problems should come as much surprise, given the developers are more content spending their time on monetization and making player skins than fixing the actual product. All I can conclude with is to announce that I'm happy to get my ten dollars back. Because when the development team can't provide a modicum of entertainment or a thoughtful experience, while spending most their time on cosmetic garbage, that's all they deserve.

I came from Backroom: Escape Together and finished it with a friend. The experience was bliss and the game isn't complete yet. Enjoyed every moment of it.  Then i sadly came to try this, and spoiler it is hot garbage. But why is that?  -First, let's wait for 4 differents intro logos to end each time just to reach the menu. What a great idea, especially after you crash. -No possibility to change the basic inputs, like moving for instance. If you play with an azerty keyboard, well good luck for you.  Having to constantly switch using [Alt + Space bar] every time you play the game/need to write something is unbelievable. Are we not in 2026? -This game weight more than 25Gb, has awful graphics, but most importantly terrible, terrible optimisation.  How can a game with a first level so empty and lacking of details/purpose/ambition still manages to put my GC on fire? -In the first level, we had to find and complete a ladder on a wall by finding pieces that doesn't even match the design of that ladder (wtf)  just to reach a key in a vent. We then died, came back there, the ladder was still assembled but for some reason we couldn't climb it.   Which means no key, so no way to reach the exit unless you restart the whole level. -In comparison, Backroom: Escape Together weights around 12Gb, has 0 bug, had stunning graphics, great levels, and refreshing gameplay/ideas. -The f****** stamina system man, i don't even want to talk about it. Designed to make sure you waste as much time as possible in repetitive levels. Big maze with ugly monster randomly waiting/teleporting in a random corner. You have stamina, you can evade. That the whole idea of the first level. -The useful ability to whistle we had in B:ET that helps locate your friend is replaced here by you showing off your badge. ?!? -In B:ET, you have a native flashlight, but here you just don't care about that apparently. Great theme (backrooms). Some levels are straight up just american neighborhoods or some creepy cellar though, which is a bit disappointing. The other "rooms" levels are liminal, and very good. The graphics and props are well made.    The game itself is horrible. Especially the monster animations and the sound effects they're producing. They're legit just screaming at you. Maybe scary if you're younger than 10 years old.     The riddles are dramatically bland and boring. Like the riddle where you have to get off of your boat, climb up a ladder, spin some valves and get back in the boat because the water has now risen and you can pass the overpass. Boom. That's it. 200 IQ. And there is almost always something trying to kill you while you solve the riddle, which only causes panic and anxiety. Not horror, or fright. Which is, I believe, what the game is trying to be.    They really tried with the whole sanity thing but all it does is tell you that you're about to die. No horror element whatsoever, and you get used to it really quickly. It's always a "shoot, I gotta restock on almond water now" moment, which feels like a chore. Almond water and protein bars are a weird choice for sanity restoration items. And what the hell is Pain Juice supposed to do?    This game feels like it's been made by a college student who has no idea how to get the idea of horror across. Someone who just found out how to use unreal engine. The only actually scary thing is that the approaching monster's footsteps become louder and louder. I urge the dev/s to try playing Amnesia to get a rough idea of horror, fright, dread and uneasiness.     I only play it because my friends like it.

[h1] This is not a "horror exploration game" [/h1]  I bought this game with the intention to explore the backrooms with my friend. What we got was a large amount of content, but in the form of a backrooms themed puzzle and occasional speed run game. Most of these levels are trial and error requiring 10-20+ attempts to figure out how the monsters behave before you can clear the level. We really tried to like this game but every session ended in an alt+f4 out of pure frustration. The backrooms are all about infinite liminal space, requiring the player to explore vastly and survive the unknown horrors. Each level was clearly boxed in and felt more gimmicky, removing exploration from the game entirely. In less than a minute you can sprint across a majority of the levels. In just a few minutes I can have the entire layout memorized. Backrooms are supposed to make you feel lost.  If you want a themed puzzle game with some jump scares this game is for you. Some of the puzzles are designed decently and there are some fun moments to be had in the game. Unfortunately, a lot of the levels result in being chased around a blind corner and quickly finding the obvious hiding spot before the entity can reach you. From there you will wait until the entity de-aggros until you can run to the next hiding spot. If you want a liminal exploration and co-op survival game, this is not it.  The store page calls this a "horror exploration game" but from my experience THIS IS A BACKROOMS THEMED, LEVEL BASED PUZZLE GAME.
If you enjoy insta-death nonsense, maybe you'll enjoy this more than I did. I think it sucks, and I think the style of gameplay on offer (in the garage in particular) is flatly not fun.

Escape the backrooms borrows from extremely fascinating internet lore, which automatically makes it a game that a lot of us are drawn to. However, I have to give negative feedback because the actual game itself is riddled with bugs, some of which game breaking, and is honestly oozing with lazy game development.  There's a game breaking bug in the laboratory after darkened suburbs where the monster never appears and you can never progress past this point. Hours of gameplay down the drain.   They can't even be bothered to include controller support even though it's generated millions of dollars in revenue. It takes 1 day to implement something like that. Just pure laziness.

Played the other major Backrooms game (Escape Together) before this one, which may have been a mistake in hindsight but also think the comparison is worth mentioning. This game seemed to be a much slower burn and didn't garner the anxiety like the other game does right away (which is fun! You buy these games for the spookiness and monster sim and being sketched out with your friends lol). The visuals were nice, but had a harder time keeping together with my friend because the rooms as SO freaking expansive you can't hear footsteps or see flashlights until they're right next to you. Items take FOREVER to discover which I think is another negative - in the first level or two you want to be discovering items to learn what their role is in your bag and which ones you would want to keep if you become over encumbered. There were also a few points where in some of the rooms or transition areas where the direction seemed odd and something as simple as what to do and then backtrack to another room was not obvious so took longer than necessary and/or were tempted to look online to see what to do next. Not good. With all the backroom games coming out, I'd hope these guys could look into improving this game because it's definitely not unplayable (I'll probably try to go through it eventually just to see all 30 rooms), just not the best one I've tried.

I'll get this out right away, DO NOT BUY UNTIL ON SALE. This game is not worth it’s full price in many ways (Read rest of review as to why)  This is a really choppy first person horror game, the quality of the game is really basic and feels cheap, and after completing doesn’t feel too memorable of a game to recommend.  The Point Of The Game. The game is a huge line up of survive/escape or find object/escape levels. This can become really repetitive especially when you are sometimes not provided the best instructions of what to do. In a way you can say a puzzle game, but it also does feel tedious after time.  The Graphics. The graphics in this game are VERY POOR. A lot of the monsters are things you can find either free or dirt cheap on Unity Store. The sounds in this game also aren’t the best with in some places they can be loud and then other times they are cutting in and out. The UI is also very basic and can be frustrating to use when you want to move items around and it drops them instead.  Voice chat Issues. Most people are getting this game to probably play with their friends (Like me). The voice chat is really basic and sometimes doesn’t even work in this game. In the beginning before we even started, in the start screen already we weren’t able to hear each other until all of us reset the game up to 2 times. During gameplay, voice chat would sometimes be very low even if you are in front of the other person talking and very bad for when using the radios as you never know if you are actually speaking or not on the radio.  Keybind Issues. This game has very basic keybinds. Only issue is with push to talk where it wouldn’t allow me to set my mouse key as a push to talk button, so I was stuck with using V.
Another game that removed VR after purchase. People who buy things for VR know that things are janky and we typically love it anyway. Bring back the VR!

I JUST FOUND OUT THIS GAME COPIES https://store.steampowered.com/app/2141730/Backrooms_Escape_Together/ HEAVILY THUS I FEEL RIPPED OFF  This game has a surprising amount of content for the price, and it nails the backroom atmosphere quite well (on high-ultra graphical settings, we'll get to that). You explore a lot of rooms, and, well, escape them, usually accompanied by a monster or multiple. The real drawback to that experience, in my eyes at least, comes from the monsters within the game.   Most monsters just chase you down, get completely stopped by a closed door. The balance SUCKS between rooms. Sometimes the room is difficult, with you dying multiple times just to figure what to do or where to go, and sometimes the exit is given to you on a golden plate, with no danger whatsoever. Rooms are mostly "collect x and/or find exit", which in theory could be interesting or engaging, but gameplay-wise is very primitive 2015-era horror walking simulator. Graphical settings degrade the experience A LOT, so if you are running a lower end machine, chances are, you will be experiencing a VASTLY different game compared to someone playing on ultra. The food/item system is not good. It's not a survival horror in a sense you don't practically need those items to beat the game (except for almond soda), and the fact you LOSE all of your items after death means you get no real implementation of a game mechanic introduced by said item afterwards. (For example, you find a radar that scans an area for an entity, but it doesn't work on other entities except one. The radar can also "paint" dark surroundings, but it doesn't do that elsewhere in the game, except level you find it at)  It's fine with friends, but for solo play it's a tedious game that offers very little.

Genuinely how does this game have this many positive reviews? Like the best thing I can say is its cool to see the different interpretations of the levels throughout the games and some levels I like visually, but wow a lot of the levels are straight up cancer to play like "FUN". Doesn't help that its a buggy mess and randomly laggy. Like for some reason when I'm low sanity I get 10 frames. I'm running the game on ultra and most of the time its fine but random parts just tanks my frames.  Speaking of sanity, dear lord it SUCKS. Especially with 4 players there is not nearly enough sanity items and it drains from 100 -> 0 in like 2 minutes, at least it feels like it. I really have no idea how nightmare is possible. I mean at the time of writing this I have 7 hours of gameplay and still have 5 levels to complete. There's also so many easy ways to die and honestly like 90% of my deaths is because of player collision, which I question why it exists here. Like I said earlier I've faced so many bugs and the game just being clunky to play doesn't help.   To complain more about odd decisions and bugs..... the lean mechanic sucks and is basically useless, stamina is dumb here and randomly decides to give you infinite stamina then takes it back, the inventory management blows, no toggle sprint & crouch, no option to disable mute when tabbed out, the lack of visual objectives, in game mics don't work on boats and randomly will bug out while speaking, low sanity = low FPS, sanity drains too much too quickly, monsters getting stuck, starting a level and having an immediate exit that gives you an achievement for "escaping", horrible menus, and just so much more..... It really makes me question how the game was released in this state, and convinces me it was neglected so they could add stupid things like player skins.  I'm bummed because I played "Backrooms: Escape Together" before this and I enjoyed my time with it but felt like there could be more. So when buying this today and seeing how many levels and the amount of positive reviews I was excited but this game just sucks in comparison. Quantity does NOT equal quality and this game proves it. Some of these levels are stupid long too.   The only reason I stuck through it thus far is because friends can make any terrible game at least a bit more fun, and without them I wouldn't of lasted an hour. Just buy a different game instead....

Escape The Backrooms is an unpolished and frustrating game that does not live up to the backrooms myth. As a Backrooms fan myself, I was left incredibly disappointed by the game, a lot of potential wasted with bad and repetitive gameplay mechanics.    - First and foremost, the game itself is quite buggy and unpolished, even years after its release ! I clipped through walls multiple times (and not to go to the backrooms !), and the physics are very junky, lagging behind, going through doors, you can tell no effort was put into making the physics smooth. Same goes for animations, some just do not work properly (for example: swimming in the air when going up stairs). Sometimes you can't interact with stuff properly, like climbing ladders etc. There's a bunch of stuff that could be improved that never got polished.    - Secondly, the monsters are just copy pasted in multiple levels. There's only like on creative mechanic with the light with the smilers and the clown, all of the other entities are just stealth over and over again. And, to top it all, if you get seen by one of the monsters, they do not leave you, the only way for them to get away is to block them. However, the mimic entity is just extremely fast, and even breaking line of sight doesn't work, you're almost dead everytime. You will just spend most of your time stealthing, crouching, and when there's an interesting and original mechanic, it will be badly executed.    - Indeed, 90% of the levels are the same mechanics: finding the exit and stealthing around the same enemies. The other 10% are actually creative ideas... except most of the time they won't be executed well. Let me give you an example: the part with the traffic lights indicating entities, and having to go in the alternate red area, is actually pretyy good ! However, sometimes, you're going to go in the upside and the traffic light will switch to orange as soon as you do, killing you. Or also, in the other area there is the mimic entity like I said which just chases you non-stop and is extremely fast. All of this could have been solved by changing the traffic lights into cameras to see who's down there and if there's actually no one there. In the end, the only truly enjoyable levels for me was the green suburbs (with the sun setting), the second version of the FUN =) level, and the Levels ! chases.    - I was playing with a friend, which made the game funnier and easier since we could continue even if someone was dead. However, I feel like playing this game solo would be extremely difficult with how random some of the events and entities can be, and also takes extremely long since some levels are just finding the exit with nothing, or going around an entire facility to collect items.    - I have to say, the levels are pretty well recreated, that's for sure. Not the best graphically but it works and I respect it for that.    - As for the story, I wish it would have went somewhere. The story is basically extremely plain: just find a way out of the Backrooms. The small bits of story about the facility and other people do not lead anywhere, and are not fleshed out at all. The Backrooms can bring so much potential with creativty, and it's a shame it was not used to its full potential.    I cannot recommend this game unfortunately, maybe if you have friends this could bring some laughs, however it will remain tedious especially in solo.

Escape the Backrooms isn’t just bad it’s the kind of release that makes you wonder how low the bar can possibly get before someone trips over it. This feels like a lazy, slapped-together mess pretending to be a game, with lifeless environments copy-pasted into oblivion and gameplay that somehow manages to be both confusing and painfully boring at the same time. Any sense of horror is completely absent; instead of tension, you’re stuck wandering through dull, empty spaces thinking about how much better literally anything else would be. It’s not creepy, it’s not clever it’s just exhausting and hollow.  And then there’s the technical side, which somehow makes everything worse. The bugs, the jank, the clumsy controls it all screams that this needed way more time, effort, and basic quality control before seeing the light of day. It gives off the impression of a project pushed out without enough care, polish, or critical self-review. There’s a difference between a rough indie experience and something that feels unfinished at its core, and this lands squarely in the latter. Instead of feeling like a passion project, it comes across as something that wasn’t ready but got released anyway, leaving players to deal with the consequences. The developers should honestly just never develop again. I want my eight dollars back.

A Broken, Overpriced Asset-Flip Simulator  Dont let the hype fool you this game is a textbook example of a millionaire developer copy-pasting free internet assets into Unreal Engine and praying it doesnt crash  Game Breaking Bugs: Basic lobby codes don't work, multiplayer connections constantly fail and game entities literally refuse to spawn, unnecessary lag and input delay  Lazy Development: The gameplay is predictable and repetitive featuring some of the worst animations and generic jumpscares in horror history. Even the "skinwalkers" just play basic prerecorded audios on a loop.  Terrible Optimization: Instead of fixing fundamental gameplay scripts or establishing a proper  functional bug-reporting system, the developer leaves the community to rely on third-party feedback sites  Save your cash. Buy Escape Together or literally any other co-op game on Steam that has actual passion and custom modeling behind it. This is a glorified broken Roblox free-model showcase sold at a premium price. 0/10

I'd like to recommend this game, but apart from the visuals, most aspects of the game feel cheap and outdated. More than wanting to recommend, I'd love to actually even like this game! First weird experience is that the size is more than twice larger than "that other competitor in the scene" whilst this looks worse ... But I wrote that off as the difference in content, scale and scope. Only played this, so for now I'm undecided on that, but I highly doubt "the other one" is worse.    Most of the entities in Escape The Backrooms look good, but they play horrible in a backrooms game. Brainless obstacles. Annoyances.  "Skin stealers"/mimics are premature with their pre-recorded excuses, then they'll chase you until the end of times, or until you go behind a door - but don't stand too close to a wall! The immersion might take you! Just kidding... The mimic can reach you through a wall.  "Smilers", at least when first encountered are utterly pointless. A note tells you to beware the dark, so you'd not even need a black cloud with a glowing face to lean you over. Just falling through the lack of light might be much more immersive, seeing how it's all just a child's play of "chicken sh!t" - "Uh oh, lights blinked! Will I make it in time or will the smiley face lean me over! So spooked!" - Those things are meant to be smart by the lore - this game reflects it in no way at all.  "Dogs"(?) are unnecessarily loud and buggy as hell. You can blind them through a brick wall and they might ignore your flashlight - and you for that matter - at any given time for reasons unknown. The stage with a lot of them itself might look good, but it doesn't FEEL like what is imprinted in my mind for said "level" by the semi-agreed upon lore. It's more like a "certain" OG Windows Screensaver, but looks too good so it's not a fit for that either. And then there's the electric puddles. That electrocute you through rubber boots. What are your hazmat suits even good for? Immersion? Oh... I'm, a reindeer.    Onto other topics, the stage "Fun!" should really need a look over. That thing is even more bugged than the hounds and is so at the size of a level. The music, the setting, the colors, the theme, the appearance of the entities are all spot-on nice. The geometry is more like a maze for the sole purpose for this game (spoiler: it IS just a maze for this game! Whoaaah!) but it cannot sustain an illusion of anything at all. No lore-fitting reason, nothing sinister, nothing alien. FFS, the pipes in Mario are more mysterious and interesting and I never even liked Mario games! Add on top that the music breaks when you pop balloons and bugs out some more when you crouch under tables, while the entities are partly-paralyzed with an intriguing but definitely buggy pattern and start growling all in a buggy choir when this happens. One can simply not mistake this for intended gameplay. Said level is impossible to beat without bugs anyway, so breaking the game becomes the game itself and it's absolutely disgraceful.    I could go on and on about this buggy mess, but maybe my shamwich of a review needs a good bottom bun: The inventory is kinda nice, apart from a few immersion-challenging bugs. Also, I will try to complete the game, even though I'm convinced my feelings toward it will be further impacted.

This game was fun because I was with my friends, the game itself is extremely, extremely buggy and there were consistent, often game breaking, errors every few minutes. Namely:  - Mics muting when sat down  - If the player you are spectating dies then your death-camera is tied to their body permanently and you cannot move or hear anything  - Sprint buttons needing to be re-toggled  - The lighting engine breaking and make the game entirely unviewable from being entirely white  - Getting stuck in objects  - Your mouse randomly not staying in the game or moving with awful lag  - Random crashes, disconnects, and freezing  - You die if you restore your sanity as it reaches 0  - The game mutes when you tab out    Outside of that the game is just poorly designed. There is no death chat meaning if three members die and one is exploring, then you sit in silence for a while until they die or finish. Additionally, there are no volume controls at all and the proximity chat has no noise filtering, so is extremely loud for regular mic settings.    The game mechanics are fine, the levels are unique in themselves but the execution is terrible. Half the puzzles are overly complex and require a guide and are just generally unfun to complete. Furthermore the Sanity Mechanic is completely useless and just annoying. It means you cut your exploration of these awesome scenes so short because you are constantly rushing to finish, and you often die whilst drinking to restore it; its also just entirely unnecessary?     It feels like a game still in alpha stage, in terms of the mass bugs but also how bad the game design is, and I would not buy this game at full price.

This game has some stunning visuals, and cool environmental design. However the actual gameplay sucks, it's like walking through mud to get from the beginning to the end. Me and a friend played through the whole game on the easiest difficulty, and it took 4 sessions to finish it, which isn't that many but we had no fun while playing. Some sections are way too difficult for their own good, and some areas really overstay their welcome. For example the pool rooms area is nice looking buy is around way too long, this is mainly due to its "puzzle" where you have to turn valves to progress on a raft. This is fine up until your being chased and killed by a really fast monster. Another problem I have with the game is that it provides items that could be used as weapons against the entities, but when you try it does nothing to them. At so many points in this game being able to at least fight off one the entities would help with the game's pacing so much but it just never really happens. Over all if your thinking of getting this game just be prepared for a glorified walking simulation game that has a bear bones survival system and really long levels, that take even longer due to a difficult enemies that will slow you down to a crawl. Please heed my warring on this game it will save you your time.
